name: Shakespeare's Plays
description: Classify Shakespeare's plays by genre and identify key characters and quotes

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Hamlet' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Hamlet is Shakespeare's longest play and arguably the most performed. The phrase 'To be, or not to be' is the most quoted line in the English language.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Macbeth' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Macbeth is Shakespeare's shortest tragedy. Actors superstitiously call it 'The Scottish Play' because saying the name inside a theater is considered bad luck.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Othello' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Othello's villain Iago is considered one of the most manipulative characters in all of literature, speaking more lines than Othello himself.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'King Lear' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: King Lear was considered so bleak that for 150 years (1681-1838) theaters only performed a rewritten version with a happy ending.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Romeo and Juliet' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Romeo and Juliet takes place over just five days. It was one of Shakespeare's most popular plays during his lifetime and has never fallen out of fashion.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Julius Caesar' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Despite being about a real historical figure, Julius Caesar is classified as a tragedy, not a history. Shakespeare's histories deal exclusively with English kings.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Antony and Cleopatra' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Antony and Cleopatra has 42 scenes -- more than any other Shakespeare play -- spanning locations from Rome to Egypt to battlefields at sea.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Coriolanus' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Coriolanus is set in ancient Rome and explores the tension between elite contempt and popular democracy. T.S. Eliot considered it superior to Hamlet.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Titus Andronicus' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Titus Andronicus is Shakespeare's bloodiest play with 14 killings, multiple mutilations, and a scene of cannibalism. It was hugely popular in Shakespeare's day but fell out of favor for centuries.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Timon of Athens' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Timon of Athens is believed to be unfinished. Karl Marx cited Timon's speeches about gold in Das Kapital as a critique of money's corrupting power.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'A Midsummer Night's Dream' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: A Midsummer Night's Dream features fairies, a love potion, and a man turned into a donkey. It remains one of the most frequently performed Shakespeare plays worldwide.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Much Ado About Nothing' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The title is likely a bawdy pun -- 'nothing' was Elizabethan slang for female anatomy, pronounced similarly to 'noting' (eavesdropping), which drives the entire plot.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Twelfth Night' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Twelfth Night features one of Shakespeare's great cross-dressing heroines, Viola. In Shakespeare's time, a boy actor would play a woman disguised as a man.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Taming of the Shrew' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Taming of the Shrew has become one of Shakespeare's most controversial plays due to its gender politics. The musical 'Kiss Me, Kate' is based on it.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'As You Like It' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: As You Like It contains the famous 'All the world's a stage' speech. The Forest of Arden is likely named after Shakespeare's mother, Mary Arden.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Merchant of Venice' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Though classified as a comedy, The Merchant of Venice is deeply uncomfortable to modern audiences due to its treatment of Shylock. The 'pound of flesh' has become a universal metaphor.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Merry Wives of Windsor' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Legend says Queen Elizabeth I so loved Falstaff that she asked Shakespeare to write a play showing him in love, resulting in this comedy written in just two weeks.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Tempest' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Tempest is believed to be the last play Shakespeare wrote alone. Prospero's final speech, renouncing his magic, is often read as Shakespeare's own farewell to the theater.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Comedy of Errors' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Comedy of Errors is Shakespeare's shortest play. It features two sets of identical twins who were separated at birth, inspired by the Roman playwright Plautus.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Love's Labour's Lost' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Love's Labour's Lost is unusual for a comedy -- it does not end with a marriage. Shakespeare reportedly wrote a sequel, Love's Labour's Won, which is now lost.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Two Gentlemen of Verona' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: This is thought to be one of Shakespeare's earliest plays. It introduced themes he would revisit throughout his career: cross-dressing heroines, banished lovers, and the forest as a place of transformation.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry V' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry V contains the famous St. Crispin's Day speech before the Battle of Agincourt. It was used as wartime propaganda in Laurence Olivier's 1944 film adaptation.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Richard III' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Richard III's opening line 'Now is the winter of our discontent' is one of Shakespeare's most famous. When Richard's actual skeleton was found under a parking lot in 2012, it confirmed Shakespeare's description of his scoliosis.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry IV, Part 1' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry IV Part 1 introduced Falstaff, who became arguably Shakespeare's most beloved comic character. Harold Bloom called Falstaff Shakespeare's greatest invention.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry IV, Part 2' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry IV Part 2 ends with the new King Henry V coldly rejecting Falstaff with 'I know thee not, old man' -- one of the most devastating moments in Shakespeare.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Richard II' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Richard II is written entirely in verse -- no prose at all. Queen Elizabeth I reportedly said 'I am Richard II, know ye not that?' believing the play was political commentary on her reign.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry VI, Part 1' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry VI Part 1 features Joan of Arc as a character. It was enormously popular when first performed, reportedly drawing 10,000 spectators over its initial run.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry VIII' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: During a performance of Henry VIII in 1613, a stage cannon set fire to the Globe Theatre's thatched roof and burned it to the ground. No one was hurt, though one man's breeches caught fire.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'King John' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Surprisingly, Shakespeare's King John never mentions the Magna Carta. The play focuses instead on the political struggle between John, the Pope, and France.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare play does the character Puck appear?
questions.explanation: Puck, also called Robin Goodfellow, is a mischievous fairy who accidentally causes a love-potion mix-up. His closing line 'If we shadows have offended' breaks the fourth wall.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare play does the character Shylock appear?
questions.explanation: Shylock is a Jewish moneylender who demands a literal pound of flesh as collateral. His speech 'Hath not a Jew eyes?' is one of Shakespeare's most powerful pleas for shared humanity.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare play does the character Prospero appear?
questions.explanation: Prospero is a sorcerer and the rightful Duke of Milan who conjures a storm to bring his enemies to his island. He is widely seen as Shakespeare's self-portrait as a storyteller.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare history play does the character Falstaff first appear?
questions.explanation: Falstaff was originally named Sir John Oldcastle, but Shakespeare changed it after Oldcastle's descendants objected. The fat, witty knight became so popular he got his own spin-off play.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare play does the villain Iago appear?
questions.explanation: Iago has the most lines of any character in Othello despite not being the title character. His motivations are so ambiguous that Samuel Taylor Coleridge called them 'motiveless malignity.'

questions.question: Which Shakespeare character delivers the 'To be, or not to be' soliloquy?
questions.explanation: This speech from Act 3, Scene 1 of Hamlet is the most famous soliloquy in Western literature. It meditates on death and suicide, asking whether suffering or oblivion is preferable.

questions.question: Which Shakespeare character says 'Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears'?
questions.explanation: Mark Antony's funeral oration in Julius Caesar is a masterclass in rhetorical manipulation. He repeatedly calls the assassins 'honorable men' while turning the crowd against them.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Winter's Tale' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Winter's Tale is often called a 'romance' or 'problem play' -- its first half is tragic, but it pivots to comedy with a 16-year time jump and the famous stage direction 'Exit, pursued by a bear.'
